title: Musicals
subtitle: Musical films across eras, types, and tones.

sections.name: I. Animated
sections.desc: Disney Renaissance, modern Disney, Pixar, non-Disney animated musicals.

items.title: The Little Mermaid
items.pitch: The film that launched the Disney Renaissance. Howard Ashman and Alan Menken's score is the template for everything that followed.

items.title: Beauty and the Beast
items.pitch: First animated film to receive a Best Picture nomination. The Beast's library scene is canon.

items.title: Aladdin
items.pitch: Robin Williams improvising as the Genie redefined what animated voice acting could be.

items.title: The Lion King
items.pitch: Hamlet on the savanna with Elton John songs. The peak of the Disney Renaissance commercially and arguably artistically.

items.title: Pocahontas
items.pitch: Ambitious historical reimagining with one of Menken's strongest scores. Politics aged complicated.

items.title: The Hunchback of Notre Dame
items.pitch: The darkest Disney Renaissance film by a wide margin. Frollo's 'Hellfire' is the most adult thing in the canon.

items.title: Hercules
items.pitch: The Muses-as-gospel-chorus framing device is the most musically interesting thing Disney did in the 90s.

items.title: Mulan
items.pitch: Disney's most action-driven musical and one of its best protagonist arcs. 'I'll Make a Man Out of You' transcends the medium.

items.title: Tarzan
items.pitch: Phil Collins replaces traditional book-style musical numbers with pop-song scoring. Transitional moment for Disney.

items.title: Anastasia
items.pitch: Don Bluth's Fox-produced rival to the Disney Renaissance. Romanov rewrite plus 'Once Upon a December' equals an underrated classic.

items.title: The Prince of Egypt
items.pitch: DreamWorks' Moses-as-blockbuster. Hans Zimmer score, ambitious animation, takes the source seriously.
items.whyPriority: Watched once, didn't rewatch. Cataloged for tracking.

items.title: West Side Story
items.pitch: Spielberg's revisit of Bernstein/Sondheim, with Tony Kushner's revised book. More faithful to the stage than the 1961 version in significant ways.

items.title: The Sound of Music
items.pitch: Julie Andrews and the Alps. Probably the most-rewatched Western family musical of the 20th century, and the form has not been improved upon.

items.title: My Fair Lady
items.pitch: Audrey Hepburn (with Marni Nixon's voice) as Eliza Doolittle. Lerner and Loewe's most enduring score.

items.title: Grease
items.pitch: Travolta and Newton-John. The 1950s through a 70s lens, by way of stage musical. 'Summer Nights' is unkillable.

items.title: Sweeney Todd: The Demon Barber of Fleet Street
items.pitch: Tim Burton's Sondheim. Depp and Bonham Carter's vocals are weak by stage standards but the visual register is right.

items.title: Les Misérables
items.pitch: Tom Hooper's live-singing experiment. Aggressively close-up shot, divisive vocally. Hathaway's 'I Dreamed a Dream' is the high point.

items.title: Into the Woods
items.pitch: Disney's Sondheim. Necessarily declawed for PG audiences but the 'Agony' duet survives intact.

items.title: Hairspray
items.pitch: John Waters by way of Broadway by way of Adam Shankman. Tracy Turnblad as relentless optimist; Travolta in drag as the joke that mostly works.

items.title: Mamma Mia!
items.pitch: ABBA jukebox film with a Greek-island setting and a cast that mostly cannot sing. Streep is committed; Brosnan is not.
items.whyPriority: Watched. Did not enjoy at all.

items.title: Annie
items.pitch: John Huston directing a musical, somehow. Carol Burnett as Miss Hannigan is the reason to watch.

items.title: Dreamgirls
items.pitch: Jennifer Hudson's 'And I Am Telling You I'm Not Going' is one of the great single-take musical-film performances. Eddie Murphy got a Best Supporting nomination.

items.title: The Phantom of the Opera
items.pitch: Joel Schumacher's full-bore Andrew Lloyd Webber. Visually committed, vocally inconsistent. Gerard Butler can't quite carry it.

items.title: The Music Man
items.pitch: Robert Preston barreling through 'Ya Got Trouble' is one of the all-time virtuoso patter performances. Meredith Willson Americana at its peak.

items.title: Wicked
items.pitch: Jon M. Chu's adaptation of the Stephen Schwartz/Winnie Holzman stage musical, itself a prequel-revisionist take on Oz. Erivo and Grande as Elphaba and Glinda. Part one of two; 'Defying Gravity' lands.
items.whyPriority: Loved. Wicked: For Good (Part 2) on the queue.

sections.name: III. Original Screen Musicals
sections.desc: Musicals written for film, not adapted from stage.

items.title: Singin' in the Rain
items.pitch: The transition-to-talkies film made by people who'd lived it. Donen and Kelly, Comden and Green. Possibly the most perfectly-constructed musical film ever made.
items.whyPriority: Personal favorite. Watched countless times. Knows every song, dance, and beat.

items.title: Mary Poppins
items.pitch: Julie Andrews's screen debut. Disney's high-water mark for live-action live-with-animation. The chimney sweep sequence is still astonishing.
items.whyPriority: Watched. Lightyears better than the sequel.

items.title: Mary Poppins Returns
items.pitch: Rob Marshall's belated sequel. Emily Blunt is well-cast but the new songs don't approach the originals. Affectionate at best.

items.title: La La Land
items.pitch: Damien Chazelle's homage to MGM musicals via modern Los Angeles. Polarizing — either you find it earnest and sweeping, or you find Stone and Gosling's voices distracting.

items.title: White Christmas
items.pitch: Crosby and Kaye in a Vermont inn. Best known for the title song; everything else is workmanlike Berlin in a holiday wrapper.

items.title: Holiday Inn
items.pitch: Where 'White Christmas' was actually introduced. Crosby and Astaire. Dated and slow by modern standards.

items.title: The Wizard of Oz
items.pitch: The original. Foundational to American musical film. Not actually as much a musical as remembered — most songs are front-loaded — but the ones that stick are immortal.

items.title: Across the Universe
items.pitch: Julie Taymor's Beatles jukebox film with a Vietnam-era backdrop. Visually inventive in spurts, narratively underbaked.

sections.name: IV. Jukebox & Bio-Musical
sections.desc: Films built around an existing songbook or a musician's life.

items.title: Pitch Perfect
items.pitch: Anna Kendrick and Rebel Wilson in a college a cappella film that earned its sequel and then some. The Riff-Off scene works every time.

items.title: Pitch Perfect 2
items.pitch: Elizabeth Banks's directorial debut. World a cappella championship plot. Sequel that improves on the formula in places, falters in others.

items.title: Pitch Perfect 3
items.pitch: USO tour plot. The trilogy's weakest installment but still charming. The Bellas finale earns the run.

items.title: The Rocky Horror Picture Show
items.pitch: Midnight-movie phenomenon. Tim Curry's Frank-N-Furter is one of the great screen performances regardless of genre. Audience participation tradition is the real text.

items.title: South Park: Bigger, Longer & Uncut
items.pitch: Trey Parker's musical-as-political-satire. Genuinely-good Broadway-style numbers ('Blame Canada' got an Oscar nomination) wrapped in foul-mouthed animation. Holds up.
